name: E2B Code Interpreter API
what_it_does: E2B provides secure, isolated cloud sandboxes for running AI-generated code. Each sandbox is a full Linux VM that spins up in ~150ms, letting agents execute Python, JavaScript, bash, and other code safely without risk to host systems. Purpose-built for AI agents — supports file uploads, persistent processes, long-running sessions (up to 24 hours), and real-time stdout/stderr streaming.
use_cases: Executing AI-generated code safely in isolated environments before deploying
Running data analysis pipelines where agents write and test Python code iteratively
Building coding assistants that need to verify their generated code actually works
Hosting interactive Jupyter-like environments for AI-driven data science
Running arbitrary user-submitted code in multi-tenant SaaS platforms
Testing and debugging AI-generated scripts without infrastructure setup
Long-running background computation tasks launched by AI agents
not_for: Persistent compute requiring GPU (use Modal or Replicate for GPU workloads)
Production server hosting — sandboxes are ephemeral compute environments
Very long-running jobs exceeding 24-hour session limits
Teams that need on-premise code execution for compliance reasons
best_when: You need an AI agent to write and run code in a loop (plan-execute-observe), especially for data analysis, coding assistants, or any workflow where validating AI-generated code before trusting it is critical. E2B's fast spin-up and Python SDK are ideal for tight agent loops.
avoid_when: You need GPU compute, persistent state across many sessions, or heavy ML model inference inside the sandbox. For pure model inference, use Replicate or Together AI instead.

alternatives.reason: Better for GPU workloads and persistent ML inference; E2B is better for short-lived code interpreter sessions
Better for running pre-built ML models; E2B is better for arbitrary agent-generated code
CI/CD code execution but not designed for low-latency agent loops

auth.notes: Single E2B_API_KEY environment variable. No OAuth, no scopes. Key passed in header or via SDK. Very simple auth model ideal for agents.

pricing.free_tier_limits: 100 hours of sandbox compute per month on free tier. No credit card required to start.
pricing.paid_tiers: Hobby: $20/month for 500 sandbox hours. Pro: $150/month for 2500 hours. Enterprise: custom. Additional usage billed at ~$0.000225/second of compute.
pricing.notes: Pricing is based on sandbox uptime (wall clock time), not CPU. Sandboxes auto-terminate on timeout. Free tier generous for development and testing.

performance.rate_limits: Free tier: 1 concurrent sandbox. Paid tiers: up to 100 concurrent sandboxes. No explicit rate limit on API calls.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: SDK raises typed exceptions with clear messages. Sandbox timeout, quota exceeded, and execution errors are distinct exception types. Stdout/stderr are captured separately from process errors.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Sandbox creation is idempotent via named sandboxes (sandbox_id). Code execution itself is not idempotent — side effects within the sandbox persist for the session lifetime.
known_agent_gotchas: Sandboxes are paused (not destroyed) when keepalive times out — resume them via sandbox_id to avoid stale state issues
File system state persists within a session but sandboxes are destroyed after 24 hours max
Package installation (pip install) inside sandbox is not cached between sessions — pre-built custom sandbox templates are needed for fast startup with dependencies
stdout/stderr streaming requires async SDK usage; synchronous polling may miss interleaved output
Concurrent sandbox limits can surprise agents that spin up many parallel sandboxes — implement a pool/semaphore pattern
Network access inside sandboxes is enabled by default — egress can be a security concern if running untrusted code
